groups.label: Legal/Patient Safety Audit Trail Network
groups.description: Attorneys, clinician-attorneys (MD/JD, RN/JD), and patient safety advocates coordinated through an advocacy network to oppose audit trail deregulation. Comments share intent signals: attorney/legal professional identity, 'pause deregulation until modern audit standards finalized' language, and opposition to ephemeral messaging. Temporal clustering shows 9 comments submitted on January 20, 2026 alone — all strongly oppose, all audit/safety focused.
fingerprint_phrases: pause deregulation until modern audit standards finalized
groups.significance: The strongest organized opposition to audit trail deregulation. Organized legal/patient safety advocacy — not commercial interest. Many are medical malpractice attorneys who use audit trails professionally. 9 comments submitted on a single day (Jan 20) indicate coordinated mobilization.
groups.org_breakdown: other: 19 (many self-identified attorneys), individual_clinician: 9 (many are clinician-attorneys: MD/JD, RN/JD), advocacy_group: 3, patient_advocate: 1, health_it_company: 1

groups.label: Health IT Vendor Pro-Deregulation Bloc
groups.description: 26 health IT companies and EHR vendors uniformly support reducing certification requirements that their products must meet. While not necessarily organized through a single campaign, these comments share talking points around FHIR/API focus, removing criteria, reducing burden, market-driven approaches, and phased transitions. The EHR Association (EHRA) and HIMSS — the vendor trade associations — filed separately with aligned positions.
groups.significance: Represents aligned commercial interest — these companies would directly benefit from reduced certification requirements. Includes Oracle Health, Amazon, athenahealth, MEDITECH, NextGen Healthcare, InterSystems, and 20 other vendors. Trade associations EHRA and HIMSS filed separately with aligned positions.

groups.label: Individual Clinician Safety Defenders
groups.description: 15 individual clinicians with direct patient care experience who strongly oppose deregulation on patient safety grounds. Significant overlap with the Legal/Patient Safety Network — 9 of these are clinician-attorneys (MD/JD, RN/JD) who appear in both campaigns. The 6 purely clinical voices without legal backgrounds add independent credibility.
groups.significance: High-credibility voices opposing deregulation on safety grounds. 9 overlap with Campaign 1 (clinician-attorneys), suggesting the patient safety advocacy community mobilized both legal and clinical professionals. 6 are purely clinical without legal ties.
groups.org_breakdown: individual_clinician: 15 (9 are also clinician-attorneys in Campaign 1)
groups.overlap_note: 9 of 15 comments overlap with Legal/Patient Safety Network (Campaign 1)

groups.label: Government Agency Opposition
groups.description: 6 of 7 government agency comments strongly oppose deregulation. While not necessarily coordinated through a single campaign, this near-unanimous institutional opposition is notable. Government agencies may independently reach similar conclusions about the risks of reducing oversight standards. Most submitted anonymously.
groups.significance: Near-unanimous government opposition (6 of 7 government comments, 86%). May reflect institutional alignment rather than organized coordination — government agencies independently recognize risks of reducing oversight.

groups.label: Coalition & Multi-Organization Letters
groups.description: 9 comments submitted on behalf of coalitions, alliances, or multi-organization networks. These single comments represent far more organizational voice than individual submissions — the LGBT Tech letters alone claim 53 co-signatories. Coalition submissions span both support and opposition positions.
groups.significance: These 9 comments represent 100+ organizations. The LGBT Tech letters (0116, 0117) claim 53 co-signatories opposing USCDI data element removal. Community Clinic Association of LA County represents 68 nonprofit health centers. Each coalition letter should be weighted differently than individual comments.

summary.methodology: Intent-based analysis: shared policy goals, organizational alignment, temporal clustering, and talking point convergence — not just text matching.
summary.overlap_note: Campaigns 1 and 3 share 9 comments (clinician-attorneys). Unique count after deduplication is 68.
